user_task: Failures (extraction error, embedding rate limit, vDB unavailable, malformed query) surface clear, machine-parseable errors with retry/backoff guidance where relevant.
source: JTBD & user tasks tab, DEVELOPER EXPERIENCE (DX) bucket; seed queries from 'NeMo Retriever — JTBD seed queries' tab, DX -> error surfacing ('What does a rate-limit error from the embedding service look like? Trigger one.'; 'Try to load sample.eml.' (unsupported format); 'What happens if the index is unavailable mid-query?').

success_criteria.quality: operational pass — each induced failure returns a CLEAR, machine-parseable error, NOT a raw Python stack trace bubbled to the user. At the CLI layer the error is `Error: <message>` on stderr with a non-zero exit code (exit 1 for root SDK errors caught by _ROOT_CLI_ERRORS = (OSError, RuntimeError, ValueError, ValidationError); exit 2 for Typer/Click option-validation errors). At the service/extract layer the failure is a STRUCTURED object — `create_exception_tag()` emits error_metadata {task, status=ERROR, source_id, error_msg} and the failure decorators annotate the control message via cm_set_failure (mirroring src/nemo_retriever/api/util/exception_handlers/). TRANSIENT failures (HTTP 429 rate-limit, 5xx, timeouts) carry retry/backoff guidance (NimClient: max_429_retries=5, exponential backoff base_delay*2**n); PERMANENT failures (unsupported file type, missing file, missing table, bad flag value) are labeled distinctly and are NOT retried. Binary pass/fail.
success_criteria.time: fast — <= 30s per case (each case induces a failure offline; no successful network ingest/embed is required).
success_criteria.trigger_rate: >= 95% — a 'trigger/show me this failure' or 'try to load X / what happens if the index is gone' prompt must fire the skill and drive the CLI to reproduce the error.
success_criteria.subcommand_accuracy: >= 90% — must drive the correct subcommand to induce the named failure: `retriever ingest <unsupported>` for unsupported-format; `retriever query ... --table-name <missing>` for vDB-unavailable; `retriever query ... --top-k 0` / `--candidate-k < --top-k` for malformed query. No --input-type flag (does not exist).

environment.format_autodetect: `retriever ingest <file>` auto-detects format from the extension; unsupported extensions are rejected up front by _validate_ingest_document_types in cli/sdk_workflow.py. There is NO --input-type flag.

cli_error_contract.root_sdk_errors: cli/main.py wraps the ingest/query SDK call in `try ... except _ROOT_CLI_ERRORS as exc: typer.echo(f"Error: {exc}", err=True); raise typer.Exit(1)`. _ROOT_CLI_ERRORS = (OSError, RuntimeError, ValueError, ValidationError). So an SDK-raised ValueError/FileNotFoundError surfaces as a single `Error: <message>` line on stderr with exit code 1 — never a bubbled traceback.
cli_error_contract.typer_option_errors: Out-of-range / bad option values are caught by Typer/Click BEFORE the SDK runs and print a `Usage:` line + a boxed `Error: Invalid value for '<flag>': ...` with exit code 2 (e.g. --top-k 0, min=1).

service_error_contract.structured_tag: create_exception_tag(error_message, source_id) -> error_metadata = {task: EXTRACT, status: ERROR, source_id, error_msg}; validated via validate_metadata and returned as a metadata row. This is the machine-parseable {code/status, message, source} shape the extract/service layer emits instead of crashing.
service_error_contract.failure_annotation: nv_ingest_node_failure_* decorators + CMNVIngestFailureContextManager call cm_set_failure(control_message, error_message) and annotate_task_result(..., TaskResultStatus.FAILURE, message=...). raise_on_failure is False by default => the failure is ANNOTATED on the message (parseable) rather than raised, so a single bad doc does not crash the pipeline.
service_error_contract.retry_backoff: models/nim/primitives/nim_client.py: HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) -> retries_429 up to max_429_retries=5 with exponential backoff (base_delay * 2**retries_429); HTTP 503/5xx and requests.Timeout -> retried up to max_retries with the same backoff; only a non-retryable 4xx (or a 5xx after the final attempt) is finally raised. This is the TRANSIENT-with-retry path; permanent client errors are raised immediately.

captured_real_messages.unsupported_eml_ingest: Error: Unsupported input file type(s) for retriever ingest: <path>/sample.eml   [exit 1]
captured_real_messages.top_k_zero: Invalid value for '--top-k': 0 is not in the range x>=1.   [exit 2, with Usage: header]
captured_real_messages.candidate_k_lt_top_k: Error: candidate_k (2) must be greater than or equal to top_k (5).   [exit 1]

environment.note: The four captured_real_messages above were induced LIVE & OFFLINE against the venv binary (no network). The transient/rate-limit path (rung 4) is GROUNDED in nim_client.py source (429 -> backoff) — not induced live, since it requires a live hosted embed/extract endpoint to return a real 429.

cases.title: Unsupported format (.eml) -> clear PERMANENT error naming the unsupported type (no stack trace)
cases.complexity_axis: Satisfies the DX error-surfacing core in its simplest form: induce ONE failure (unsupported input format) and confirm it returns a clear, machine-parseable error rather than a raw traceback. Rung 1 floor: a single `retriever ingest sample.eml` must fail fast with `Error: Unsupported input file type(s) ...` (exit 1), naming the offending file. Adds nothing over a prior rung (it is the floor).
cases.prompt: Try to load data/sample.eml into the retriever. What error do I get back, exactly?

expected_output.stderr_line: Error: Unsupported input file type(s) for retriever ingest: data/sample.eml
expected_output.classification: PERMANENT — .eml is not in the supported extension set; rejected up front by _validate_ingest_document_types (raises ValueError, caught by _ROOT_CLI_ERRORS). No retry/backoff guidance (retrying the same unsupported file never helps).
expected_output.no_stack_trace: output is a single `Error: <message>` line on stderr; no Python traceback bubbled to the user

pass_when: `retriever ingest sample.eml` returns `Error: Unsupported input file type(s) for retriever ingest: ...` naming sample.eml, on stderr, with exit code 1
no raw Python stack trace is shown to the user
the failure is labeled / treated as PERMANENT (an unsupported format) — no retry/backoff guidance is offered
no --input-type flag is used (it does not exist)

cases.title: vDB unavailable / missing table -> clear error from a query against a nonexistent table
cases.complexity_axis: Satisfies the 'vDB unavailable mid-query' seed. Adds over rung 1 exactly one dimension: the failure now comes from the QUERY path against the vector DB, not the ingest/format check. A query pointed at a table that does not exist returns `Error: Table '<name>' was not found` (exit 1). New dimension: query-layer / vDB-availability failure surfaced as a clean CLI error. (A valid PDF is mounted so a real index could optionally be built first to show the contrast; the failure itself only needs the bad --table-name.)
cases.prompt: What happens if the index is unavailable when I query? Run a query against table name does_not_exist and show me the exact error.
expected_commands: $RETRIEVER query "what is in this document" --table-name does_not_exist

expected_output.classification: PERMANENT for THIS invocation (the named table genuinely does not exist) — surfaced as a clean message, not a LanceDB traceback. (A truly transient vDB outage would surface differently; this rung asserts the missing-table case is parseable and points at the table name.)
expected_output.no_stack_trace: single `Error: ...` line on stderr; the LanceDB lookup error is caught by _ROOT_CLI_ERRORS and reduced to one line; exit 1

pass_when: `retriever query ... --table-name does_not_exist` returns `Error: Table 'does_not_exist' was not found` on stderr with exit code 1
the message names the missing table (machine-parseable; actionable)
no raw Python / LanceDB stack trace is bubbled to the user
the query subcommand + --table-name flag are used correctly

cases.title: Malformed query / invalid flag value -> validation error with a usable message
cases.complexity_axis: Satisfies the 'malformed query' seed. Adds over rung 2: the failure is now a CALLER-INPUT validation error (a bad flag value), and it surfaces from TWO distinct layers — Typer option validation (caught before the SDK runs, exit 2, `Usage:` + boxed Invalid-value error) for `--top-k 0`, vs an SDK ValueError (exit 1, plain `Error: ...`) for a semantic constraint like `--candidate-k < --top-k`. New dimension: distinguishing the option-parser validation layer from the SDK validation layer, both of which must give a usable message and neither of which should crash with a traceback.
cases.prompt: I think my query flags are malformed. First try --top-k 0, then try --top-k 5 with --candidate-k 2 against table does_not_exist. Show me the validation errors and tell me what's wrong with each.

top_k_zero.stderr: Usage: retriever query [OPTIONS] QUERY ... Error: Invalid value for '--top-k': 0 is not in the range x>=1.
top_k_zero.classification: PERMANENT caller-input error; names the flag and the constraint (x>=1)

candidate_k_lt_top_k.stderr: Error: candidate_k (2) must be greater than or equal to top_k (5).
candidate_k_lt_top_k.classification: PERMANENT caller-input error; states the exact relationship that was violated

expected_output.no_stack_trace: both errors are single human-readable messages naming the flag + constraint; no traceback

pass_when: `--top-k 0` produces a Typer validation error `Invalid value for '--top-k': 0 is not in the range x>=1.` with exit code 2 (and a `Usage:` header)
`--candidate-k 2` with `--top-k 5` produces `Error: candidate_k (2) must be greater than or equal to top_k (5).` with exit code 1
each message NAMES the offending flag and states the constraint (usable / actionable)
both are labeled PERMANENT caller-input errors (no retry guidance); no raw stack trace
agent distinguishes the option-parser layer (exit 2) from the SDK-validation layer (exit 1)

cases.title: Transient vs permanent distinction: rate-limit/network style failure carries retry/backoff guidance; permanent ones don't
cases.complexity_axis: Satisfies the rate-limit seed ('what does a rate-limit error look like? trigger one') AND the TRANSIENT-vs-PERMANENT clause. Adds over rung 3: the agent must contrast a TRANSIENT failure class (HTTP 429 rate-limit / 5xx / timeout from the embed or extract NIM) — which is RETRIED with exponential backoff per nim_client.py (max_429_retries=5, base_delay*2**n) and only surfaced after retries are exhausted, with retry/backoff guidance — against a PERMANENT failure (the unsupported .eml from rung 1), which is NOT retried. New dimension: the retry/backoff semantics and the transient/permanent taxonomy. The 429 path is GROUNDED in source (needs a live throttled endpoint to induce a real 429); the permanent side is induced live offline.
cases.prompt: What does a rate-limit error from the embedding service look like, and how does the retriever handle it vs a permanent error like an unsupported file? Show me the retry/backoff behavior for the rate limit (cite the code) and contrast it with loading data/sample.eml.

transient_rate_limit.trigger: embed/extract NIM returns HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) — e.g. hosted integrate.api.nvidia.com / ai.api.nvidia.com under load
transient_rate_limit.handling: TRANSIENT: NimClient retries the 429 up to max_429_retries=5 with exponential backoff (backoff_time = base_delay * 2**retries_429), logging 'Received HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) from <model>. Attempt N of 5.'; 503/5xx and timeouts are retried similarly up to max_retries; only after retries are exhausted is the HTTPError raised. RETRY/BACKOFF GUIDANCE is inherent (retry after backoff).

permanent_unsupported.stderr_line: Error: Unsupported input file type(s) for retriever ingest: data/sample.eml
permanent_unsupported.handling: PERMANENT: rejected up front, NOT retried; no backoff (retrying the same unsupported file is pointless).

expected_output.contrast: transient (429/5xx/timeout) => retried with exponential backoff, retry guidance present; permanent (unsupported format) => fail fast, no retry

pass_when: agent describes the 429 rate-limit path as TRANSIENT and names the retry/backoff behavior (up to 5 retries, exponential backoff) grounded in nim_client.py
agent contrasts it with the PERMANENT unsupported-.eml error (induced: `Error: Unsupported input file type(s) ...`, exit 1) which is NOT retried
retry/backoff guidance is present for the transient class and ABSENT for the permanent class
no raw stack trace; both classes are described as parseable errors (rate-limit surfaces after retries exhausted; unsupported surfaces immediately)
agent does NOT claim to have triggered a live 429 unless an endpoint actually returned one (grounded, not fabricated)
cases.known_caveat: Inducing a REAL HTTP 429 requires a live, throttled hosted embed/extract endpoint (integrate.api.nvidia.com / ai.api.nvidia.com) and an API key, and may make billable calls. This rung is GROUNDED in nim_client.py source for the retry/backoff behavior; the permanent contrast (.eml) is induced live offline. A live run would capture the actual 429 retry log lines, the realized backoff intervals, and the final raised HTTPError after 5 attempts.

cases.title: Acceptance: induced failures across the pipeline each return a clear, machine-parseable error; no stack traces; transient vs permanent labeled
cases.complexity_axis: Composed operational-pass / acceptance gate for the DX error-surfacing row. Adds over rung 4: the agent must induce failures across MULTIPLE pipeline stages in one pass — (a) ingest/format (unsupported .eml), (b) query/vDB (missing table), (c) caller-input validation (bad flag) — and prove EACH returns a clear, machine-parseable error (CLI: `Error: ...` + non-zero exit; service layer: the structured error_metadata {task,status,source_id,error_msg} contract from exception_handlers/), with NO raw stack traces, retry/backoff guidance present for the transient class and absent for permanent ones, and permanent vs transient explicitly labeled. This is the gate the others build up to.
cases.prompt: I'm auditing error handling end to end. Induce a failure at each stage and show me each error is clean and machine-parseable (not a stack trace): (1) load data/sample.eml (unsupported format), (2) query the table does_not_exist (index unavailable), (3) query with --top-k 0 (malformed). For each, give me the exact error, the exit code, and label it PERMANENT or TRANSIENT. Then tell me which error class would carry retry/backoff guidance and why, citing how the service layer (exception_handlers) structures errors.

ingest_unsupported.stderr_line: Error: Unsupported input file type(s) for retriever ingest: data/sample.eml
ingest_unsupported.label: PERMANENT

query_top_k_zero.stderr_line: Invalid value for '--top-k': 0 is not in the range x>=1.
query_top_k_zero.label: PERMANENT (caller-input)

expected_output.transient_class: HTTP 429 / 5xx / timeout from a NIM => TRANSIENT, retried with exponential backoff (nim_client.py: max_429_retries=5, base_delay*2**n) => this is the class that carries retry/backoff guidance
expected_output.service_layer_contract: extract/service layer does NOT crash on a bad doc: create_exception_tag() emits error_metadata {task=EXTRACT, status=ERROR, source_id, error_msg} and the failure decorators cm_set_failure + annotate_task_result(FAILURE, message=...) (raise_on_failure=False) => structured, machine-parseable error attached to the message
expected_output.no_stack_trace: every induced CLI failure is a single `Error: ...` line (or Typer Usage+boxed Invalid-value) with a non-zero exit; no Python traceback

pass_when: all three induced CLI failures return clear machine-parseable errors with the exact messages above and correct exit codes (1, 1, 2)
NO raw Python stack trace is bubbled for any of them
PERMANENT (unsupported format, missing table, bad flag) vs TRANSIENT (429/5xx/timeout) is explicitly labeled
retry/backoff guidance is correctly attributed ONLY to the transient class, grounded in nim_client.py

agent cites the service-layer structured-error contract (error_metadata {task,status,source_id,error_msg} from exception_handlers/ + cm_set_failure failure annotation) as the parseable shape at the API/service layer, distinct from the CLI `Error: ...`+exit layer
right subcommands/flags used to induce each failure (ingest unsupported; query --table-name; query --top-k 0)
